experiences.intentions:
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from proposals alone; as a Servant on round 1 quest 1, a common good strategy is to approve an initial 2-player team absent strong reads, since early data is limited and rejecting gives little information. My vote reveals I’m not overreacting without evidence and helps advance the game.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil, so team [0,1] contains no known evil and is the best available 2-player opener from my perspective. In team voting, good should generally approve clean teams early to secure a success and avoid unnecessary rejection information that can help evil steer. Approving reveals little beyond support for a strong team, which benefits good more than it hurts.
Player 0: No concrete role information yet from quest history or votes; as a Servant on round 1, a standard approach is to approve the initial 2-person team including myself because it gathers early information and has a decent chance to succeed. Rejecting provides little value this early, and my vote reveals only a normal willingness to test the first team, which does not significantly help evil.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are good. The current phase is team voting on quest 1 for a 2-player team [0,1], which contains no evil players, so if it goes it will likely succeed and give good an early clean quest. As evil, I prefer to reject clean teams early to push toward teams that include me or 2, while a reject vote reveals little because early votes are noisy and not strongly alignment-indicative.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are unknown good roles. The current phase is team voting on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,1] contains no evil players, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives good an early clear. As evil, I prefer to reject a clean team to avoid helping good build trust and to push for teams including me or my teammate later. My vote itself reveals little on round 1 since early rejections are common and can be justified as skepticism of a 2-person opener.

Approving reveals I support continuity from a successful team, which helps good more than hurt.
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so 0 and 1 are somewhat more trusted, though a success does not clear them completely. Player 1 now adds 3 to make a 3-person team for Quest 2; with no strong evidence against 3, this is a reasonable progression. As a Good Servant on team voting, optimal play is to approve teams that include previously successful members and avoid unnecessary rejections early. My vote reveals I am willing to back a sensible low-risk team, which helps Good more than rejecting without evidence.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,3 are all ostensibly good from my perspective. The proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is extremely likely to succeed and further confirm a block of good players while giving evil no influence on Quest 2. As Assassin on evil, optimal play is to reject clean good teams, especially early, to seek inclusion of at least one evil player on future teams and avoid strengthening a trusted good core. My reject slightly signals suspicion of this all-good team, but that's acceptable since evil generally wants to block teams without evil presence.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are Evil, so team [0,1,3] excludes both known Evil players and is very likely all Good. In team voting, approving a clean team is optimal for Good, and as Merlin I should support successful quests without revealing too much. Approving is consistent with normal Good play and does not leak unique information beyond favoring a strong team.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,3] contains no evil players and would very likely succeed, giving Good another point. The previous successful team [0,1] gives little alignment info because evil can pass early, but from my perspective the optimal evil play in team voting is to reject all-good teams when possible. My reject vote signals little because a minority can oppose for many reasons, but approving would help Good secure a clean second success.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [2,4] is entirely evil, so approving would almost certainly hand evil a point on quest 3. As good, optimal play is to reject immediately. My vote reveals some suspicion of them, but rejecting an all-evil team is necessary and still ambiguous enough in a 5-player game.
Player 0: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3] suggest players 0,1,3 are more likely good, though not confirmed. The proposed team [2,4] excludes all previously tested successful members and puts two untested players together on the pivotal third quest. As a good Servant in team voting, the optimal play is to reject risky, low-information teams when a safer team built from prior successes exists. Rejecting does not reveal much beyond preferring evidence-based teams, and it helps the good side avoid giving evil a strong chance to score.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [2,4] is double evil. The first two quests succeeded on all-good teams, so good likely trusts 0/1 and maybe 3 by association. In team voting, as evil on quest 3 down 0-2, the optimal play is to approve any team containing enough evil to fail, especially a 2-player team with both evils. Approving may look somewhat suspicious, but rejecting would risk losing a guaranteed fail opportunity and gives no benefit to evil.
Player 2: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and 0/1/3 are good. The proposed team [2,4] is all evil, which gives us maximum control. In team voting as evil, I want this team approved because quest 3 is pivotal and a 2-player evil team can fail the mission. Approving also looks somewhat self-interested as leader but is still necessary; rejecting would hurt evil's best chance to get on mission.
Player 3: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3] mean players 0,1,3 are collectively very likely good, though one evil could still be hiding by choosing pass. Proposed team [2,4] excludes all proven-success participants and puts two untested players together on quest 3. As a Good servant in voting phase, optimal play is to reject risky teams that discard stronger evidence-based options. Rejecting reveals preference for evidence-backed teams and helps good avoid handing evil a free chance to fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: Two successful quests used teams [0,1] and [0,1,3], so 0 and 1 look strongly good, and I know I am good. Team [0,1] is a conservative high-confidence pair for quest 3 in a 5-player game where mission 3 still only needs one fail to fail. As a good servant, I should approve strong teams built from proven successes. Approving also preserves pressure on the likely suspicious outsiders 2 and 4 rather than introducing uncertainty.
Player 0: We know quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3], so players 0 and 1 look strongly good, while 3 is somewhat cleared from quest 2. As a good Servant in team voting on quest 3, approving a team of two proven-success players is strong, even though quest 3 normally needs 3 members; given the prompt asks only to vote on the proposed [0,1], I should support the safest-looking pair. Approving reveals consistency with backing successful teams and does not meaningfully hurt Good.
Player 2: Players 0 and 1 have been on both successful quests, which makes them look good to the table, though as evil I know at least one of 2/4 must get onto a team soon to recover. Current phase is team voting on [0,1], a 2-player team for quest 3, which is invalid for the actual quest size and even if treated as the proposal at hand, approving another clean-looking good team risks a third success and immediate loss pressure. My vote itself reveals little since evil should generally oppose all-good teams; rejecting helps prevent good from cruising and preserves flexibility for later rounds.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. Team [0,1] excludes known evil players and already succeeded on quest 1, so it is a very safe pair. On quest 3 with a 2-person team in this 5-player setup, approving preserves a strong good lineup and avoids unnecessary rejections. My vote aligns with good interests and does not reveal extra Merlin-specific information beyond supporting successful teams.
Player 4: Players 0 and 1 were both on the first two successful quests, so from public info they look very good; as evil Assassin, letting a third success with [0,1] would put evil in a must-win hole at 0-3. Since neither evil player is on this proposed team, approving gives good a clean chance to reach match point. Rejecting is optimal to prevent another guaranteed success team and push for teams including 2 or 4 later. My reject reveals little because evil should oppose strong all-good cores anyway.
